message.content:
Implement the following plan:

# Plan: Break `.epic/git.md` into implementable issues

## Context

The `.epic/git.md` file is a comprehensive architectural reference for building a Git abstraction layer in cupel, modeled after Zed's git crates. Phase 0 of cupel has 3 goals:

1. Pure git abstraction layer (wrapper around git2, libgit2, git CLI, gh CLI)
2. Abstraction layer around GitStore and repository entity
3. Buffer git diff state with listener on up-to-date diff hunks

The project is brand new — `src/git.rs` and `src/app.rs` are empty stubs. We need to decompose the epic into a parent issue with 12 child issues written to `.issues/`.

## Approach

Create a parent feature issue `feature-git-abstraction-layer.md` with 12 child issues organized across 3 layers:

### Layer 1 — Pure Git Abstraction (issues 1–7)
1. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--module-scaffolding.md` — Add git2/imara-diff deps, create `src/git/` module layout
2. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--data-types.md` — RepoPath, StatusEntry, Branch, BlameEntry, etc.
3. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--error-types.md` — GitError enum with thiserror
4. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--repository-trait.md` — GitRepository trait definition (~40 methods)
5. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--real-repo-readonly.md` — RealGitRepository read-only methods (status, branch, blame, log)
6. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--real-repo-mutating.md` — RealGitRepository mutating + CLI methods (stage, commit, push, pull)
7. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--fake-repository.md` — FakeGitRepository in-memory test double

### Layer 2 — GitStore & Repository Entities (issues 8–10)
8. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--repository-entity.md` — Repository GPUI entity with async job queue
9. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--git-store-entity.md` — GitStore GPUI entity as repository registry
10. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--events-optimistic-ui.md` — GitStoreEvent enum + pending_ops optimistic UI

### Layer 3 — Buffer Diff (issues 11–12)
11. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--buffer-diff-entity.md` — BufferDiff entity with imara-diff recalculation
12. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--secondary-hunk-status.md` — SecondaryHunkStatus diff-of-diffs for staging awareness

## Dependency Graph

```
#1 (scaffolding)
├── #2 (data types)  ─┐
├── #3 (error types) ─┤
│                      ├── #4 (trait) ──┬── #5 (real readonly) ── #6 (real mutating) ──┐
│                      │                └── #7 (fake repo) ────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                                                                ├── #8 (repo entity) ── #9 (git store) ── #10 (events/optimistic)
└── #11 (buffer diff, only needs imara-diff dep) ── #12 (secondary hunk status, also needs #10)
```

## Execution

Use the issue_writer skill template for each issue. Write all 13 files (1 parent + 12 children) to `.issues/`.

## Verification

- All 13 files exist in `.issues/`
- Parent references all children
- Children reference parent
- Cross-links between children use correct filenames
- Each issue has: Summary, type-appropriate template sections, Test Guidance, Definition of Done


If you need specific details from before exiting plan mode (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/denny/.claude/projects/-Users-denny-Documents-repos-cupel/49b8c445-3ace-4435-88eb-729b7bc67138.jsonl

# Plan: Break `.epic/git.md` into implementable issues

## Context

The `.epic/git.md` file is a comprehensive architectural reference for building a Git abstraction layer in cupel, modeled after Zed's git crates. Phase 0 of cupel has 3 goals:

1. Pure git abstraction layer (wrapper around git2, libgit2, git CLI, gh CLI)
2. Abstraction layer around GitStore and repository entity
3. Buffer git diff state with listener on up-to-date diff hunks

The project is brand new — `src/git.rs` and `src/app.rs` are empty stubs. We need to decompose the epic into a parent issue with 12 child issues written to `.issues/`.

## Approach

Create a parent feature issue `feature-git-abstraction-layer.md` with 12 child issues organized across 3 layers:

### Layer 1 — Pure Git Abstraction (issues 1–7)
1. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--module-scaffolding.md` — Add git2/imara-diff deps, create `src/git/` module layout
2. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--data-types.md` — RepoPath, StatusEntry, Branch, BlameEntry, etc.
3. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--error-types.md` — GitError enum with thiserror
4. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--repository-trait.md` — GitRepository trait definition (~40 methods)
5. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--real-repo-readonly.md` — RealGitRepository read-only methods (status, branch, blame, log)
6. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--real-repo-mutating.md` — RealGitRepository mutating + CLI methods (stage, commit, push, pull)
7. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--fake-repository.md` — FakeGitRepository in-memory test double

### Layer 2 — GitStore & Repository Entities (issues 8–10)
8. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--repository-entity.md` — Repository GPUI entity with async job queue
9. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--git-store-entity.md` — GitStore GPUI entity as repository registry
10. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--events-optimistic-ui.md` — GitStoreEvent enum + pending_ops optimistic UI

### Layer 3 — Buffer Diff (issues 11–12)
11. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--buffer-diff-entity.md` — BufferDiff entity with imara-diff recalculation
12. `feature-git-abstraction-layer--secondary-hunk-status.md` — SecondaryHunkStatus diff-of-diffs for staging awareness

## Dependency Graph

```
#1 (scaffolding)
├── #2 (data types)  ─┐
├── #3 (error types) ─┤
│                      ├── #4 (trait) ──┬── #5 (real readonly) ── #6 (real mutating) ──┐
│                      │                └── #7 (fake repo) ────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                                                                ├── #8 (repo entity) ── #9 (git store) ── #10 (events/optimistic)
└── #11 (buffer diff, only needs imara-diff dep) ── #12 (secondary hunk status, also needs #10)
```

## Execution

Use the issue_writer skill template for each issue. Write all 13 files (1 parent + 12 children) to `.issues/`.

## Verification

- All 13 files exist in `.issues/`
- Parent references all children
- Children reference parent
- Cross-links between children use correct filenames
- Each issue has: Summary, type-appropriate template sections, Test Guidance, Definition of Done

     4→> **Purpose of this document:** A thorough implementation reference for the three supporting crates that underpin the Git Panel. It covers every major type, trait, data structure, and design decision, with rationale and Rust code skeletons oriented toward replicating the pattern in a private GPUI project.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## How the Four Crates Relate

/Users/denny/Documents/repos/cupel/.claude/skill/issue_writer/SKILL.md

1→---
     2→name: issue_writer
     3→description: >
     4→  Generate structured, type-aware issue markdown files from task descriptions.
     5→  Use this skill whenever the user asks to create issues, tickets, tasks, bug reports,
     6→  feature requests, or any project-tracking artifact written as markdown. Also trigger
     7→  when the user says things like "write me an issue for…", "break this work down",
     8→  "I need to track this task", "document this bug", "create a ticket", or describes
     9→  work that needs scoping, splitting, or formal specification. The skill covers scope
    10→  assessment, issue-type classification, template selection, test guidance, and
    11→  Definition of Done — and writes all output into a `.issues/` directory at the
    12→  project root.
    13→---
    14→
    15→# Issue Writer
    16→
    17→Write comprehensive, agent-readable issue markdown files from raw task descriptions.
    18→Every issue is self-contained: an agent (or human) picking it up should understand
    19→the problem, the shape of the solution, how to verify it, and when it is done —
    20→without reading anything else.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## 1 — Output location and naming
    25→
    26→Store every generated issue file inside a `.issues/` directory at the **root** of the
    27→project (create it if it does not exist).
    28→
    29→### Naming convention
    30→
    31→```
    32→.issues/<type>-<slug>.md
    33→```
    34→
    35→- `<type>` is one of: `bug`, `feature`, `refactor`, `debt`, `docs`, `perf`.
    36→- `<slug>` is a lowercase, hyphen-separated summary (3–6 words max).
    37→
    38→**Examples:**
    39→
    40→```
    41→.issues/bug-null-pointer-on-empty-input.md
    42→.issues/feature-user-export-csv.md
    43→.issues/refactor-extract-auth-middleware.md
    44→```
    45→
    46→When a task is split into a parent with children, create:
    47→
    48→```
    49→.issues/feature-payment-flow.md              ← parent
    50→.issues/feature-payment-flow--api-design.md  ← child
    51→.issues/feature-payment-flow--ui-form.md     ← child
    52→.issues/bug-payment-flow--rounding-error.md  ← child (different type is OK)
    53→```
    54→
    55→The double-hyphen `--` separates the parent slug from the child slug. Children
    56→reference the parent file path in their front matter.
    57→
    58→---
    59→
    60→## 2 — Scope assessment: single issue or split?
    61→
    62→Before writing anything, evaluate scope. The goal is that each issue represents a
    63→single reviewable, testable, deployable unit of work.
    64→
    65→### Heuristics for splitting
    66→
    67→Consider splitting when **two or more** of these are true:
    68→
    69→| Signal                        | Indicator                                                              |
    70→| ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
    71→| **Distinct concerns**         | The task touches unrelated subsystems (e.g., API + UI + DB migration). |
    72→| **Multiple actors**           | Different people or teams would naturally own different parts.         |
    73→| **Independent deployability** | Parts could ship and deliver value on their own.                       |
    74→| **Estimated effort**          | Total effort exceeds roughly 1–2 working days for one person.          |
    75→| **Dependency chain**          | One part blocks another — explicit ordering helps.                     |
    76→| **Mixed types**               | The task blends a bug fix with a new feature or refactoring.           |
    77→
    78→If only zero or one signal fires, keep it as a single issue.
    79→
    80→### When you split
    81→
    82→1. Create a **parent issue** that describes the overall objective and lists every
    83→   child with its file path.
    84→2. Create one **child issue per concern**. Each child carries its own type, template,
    85→   tests, and Definition of Done.
    86→3. In the parent, include a section called `## Child Issues` with a checklist:
    87→   ```markdown
    88→   ## Child Issues
    89→
    90→   - [ ] [API design](feature-payment-flow--api-design.md)
    91→   - [ ] [UI form](feature-payment-flow--ui-form.md)
    92→   - [ ] [Fix rounding](bug-payment-flow--rounding-error.md)
    93→   ```
    94→4. In each child, add front-matter or a header line:
    95→   ```markdown
    96→   **Parent:** [Payment Flow](feature-payment-flow.md)
    97→   ```
    98→
    99→---
   100→
   101→## 3 — Classifying the issue type
   102→
   103→Determine the type from the task description before selecting a template. This
   104→table maps common language cues to issue types:
   105→
   106→| Type         | Typical cues in the description                                                                                         |
   107→| ------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   108→| **bug**      | "broken", "crash", "incorrect output", "regression", "doesn't work", error messages, stack traces, unexpected behaviour |
   109→| **feature**  | "add", "new capability", "user should be able to", "support for", "as a user I want"                                    |
   110→| **refactor** | "clean up", "restructure", "extract", "rename", "simplify", "reduce coupling" — no observable behaviour change          |
   111→| **debt**     | "upgrade dependency", "remove deprecated API", "fix linter warnings", "address TODOs", "migrate to new library"         |
   112→| **docs**     | "document", "README", "API reference", "add examples", "onboarding guide"                                               |
   113→| **perf**     | "slow", "timeout", "optimise", "reduce memory", "latency", benchmark numbers                                            |
   114→
   115→### Disambiguation rules for ambiguous tasks
   116→
   117→Some tasks straddle types. Apply these tie-breakers in order:
   118→
   119→1. **Behaviour is currently wrong** → `bug`, even if the fix also improves code
   120→   structure.
   121→2. **External behaviour changes for the user** → `feature`, even if it also
   122→   cleans things up internally.
   123→3. **Performance is the primary goal** → `perf`, even if the approach is
   124→   restructuring code.
   125→4. **No external behaviour changes at all** → `refactor` if the code is being
   126→   reshaped, `debt` if the change is about maintenance hygiene (dependency
   127→   bumps, warning suppression, config migration).
   128→5. **The deliverable is prose, not code** → `docs`.
   129→
   130→If genuinely uncertain after these steps, pick the type whose template captures
   131→the most useful information for the task and note the ambiguity in the issue body
   132→under a `> **Classification note:**` blockquote.
   133→
   134→---
   135→
   136→## 4 — Templates by issue type
   137→
   138→Every issue begins with a common header, then diverges by type.
   139→
   140→### 4.0 — Common header (all types)
   141→
   142→```markdown
   143→# <Title>
   144→
   145→| Field        | Value         |
   146→| ------------ | ------------- |
   147→| **Type**     | `<type>`      |
   148→| **Priority** | `<p0–p3>`     |
   149→| **Parent**   | (path or "—") |
   150→| **Created**  | <YYYY-MM-DD>  |
   151→
   152→## Summary
   153→
   154→A 1–3 sentence plain-language description of what this issue is about and why it
   155→matters.
   156→```
   157→
   158→Priority levels:
   159→
   160→- **p0** — System down or data loss; drop everything.
   161→- **p1** — Significant impact; address within the current sprint.
   162→- **p2** — Important but not urgent; schedule soon.
   163→- **p3** — Nice-to-have; backlog.
   164→
   165→---
   166→
   167→### 4.1 — Bug
   168→
   169→```markdown
   170→## Environment
   171→
   172→Runtime, OS, language version, relevant dependency versions.
   173→
   174→## Steps to Reproduce
   175→
   176→1. …
   177→2. …
   178→3. …
   179→
   180→## Expected Behaviour
   181→
   182→What should happen.
   183→
   184→## Actual Behaviour
   185→
   186→What happens instead. Include error messages, logs, or screenshots if available.
   187→
   188→## Root Cause Analysis
   189→
   190→If the cause is known or suspected, describe it here. Otherwise write
   191→"To be investigated."
   192→
   193→## Proposed Fix
   194→
   195→High-level approach to the fix (not a full implementation plan).
   196→```
   197→
   198→---
   199→
   200→### 4.2 — Feature
   201→
   202→```markdown
   203→## User Story
   204→
   205→As a <role>, I want <capability>, so that <benefit>.
   206→
   207→## Detailed Description
   208→
   209→Expand on the user story: context, workflows affected, edge cases to consider.
   210→
   211→## Success Criteria
   212→
   213→- [ ] Criterion 1 — observable, verifiable outcome.
   214→- [ ] Criterion 2
   215→- …
   216→
   217→## Design Considerations
   218→
   219→API contracts, UI wireframe notes, data model changes, or architectural
   220→decisions. Link to external design docs if they exist.
   221→
   222→## Out of Scope
   223→
   224→Explicitly list things this issue does NOT cover to prevent scope creep.
   225→```
   226→
   227→---
   228→
   229→### 4.3 — Refactor
   230→
   231→```markdown
   232→## Current State
   233→
   234→Describe the code or architecture as it exists today and why it is problematic.
   235→
   236→## Target State
   237→
   238→Describe the desired structure after the refactor.
   239→
   240→## Migration Strategy
   241→
   242→Step-by-step approach to move from current to target without breaking things.
   243→Favour incremental steps that keep the build green.
   244→
   245→## Behavioural Invariants
   246→
   247→List the external behaviours that must remain unchanged after this refactor.
   248→These become your regression-test anchors.
   249→```
   250→
   251→---
   252→
   253→### 4.4 — Technical Debt
   254→
   255→```markdown
   256→## Debt Description
   257→
   258→What the debt is and how it accumulated.
   259→
   260→## Impact
   261→
   262→Concrete consequences of leaving this debt in place (slower builds, security
   263→exposure, blocked upgrades, developer friction).
   264→
   265→## Remediation Plan
   266→
   267→Ordered steps to pay down the debt.
   268→
   269→## Rollback Considerations
   270→
   271→What to do if the remediation causes unexpected problems.
   272→```
   273→
   274→---
   275→
   276→### 4.5 — Documentation
   277→
   278→```markdown
   279→## Audience
   280→
   281→Who will read this documentation (end-users, developers, ops, etc.).
   282→
   283→## Scope
   284→
   285→Which topics or APIs the documentation covers.
   286→
   287→## Outline
   288→
   289→Proposed structure / table of contents for the documentation deliverable.
   290→
   291→## Source Material
   292→
   293→Links to code, existing docs, conversations, or specs that inform the content.
   294→```
   295→
   296→---
   297→
   298→### 4.6 — Performance
   299→
   300→```markdown
   301→## Current Performance
   302→
   303→Measurements or observations: latency, throughput, memory, CPU, bundle size.
   304→Include how measurements were taken (tool, environment, dataset).
   305→
   306→## Target Performance
   307→
   308→Quantitative targets that define success (e.g., "p99 latency < 200ms under 1k
   309→concurrent requests").
   310→
   311→## Hypothesis
   312→
   313→What you believe is causing the bottleneck and why.
   314→
   315→## Proposed Approach
   316→
   317→Optimisation strategy — algorithmic change, caching, parallelism, resource
   318→pooling, etc.
   319→
   320→## Benchmark Plan
   321→
   322→How performance will be measured before and after to validate improvement.
   323→```
   324→
   325→---
   326→
   327→## 5 — Test definition
   328→
   329→Every issue includes a `## Test Guidance` section. The content varies by type —
   330→the aim is to tell the implementer _what kinds_ of tests to write, not to supply
   331→code.
   332→
   333→### Principles
   334→
   335→- Describe the **category** of test and what it should exercise, not the
   336→  implementation.
   337→- Be specific about **boundary conditions** and **critical paths** worth testing.
   338→- Mention any infrastructure the tests will need (fixtures, mocks, staging
   339→  environment, seed data).
   340→
   341→### Type-specific guidance
   342→
   343→| Type         | Primary test category         | Focus areas                                                                                                                           |
   344→| ------------ | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   345→| **bug**      | Unit tests                    | Reproduce the exact failing case first. Cover surrounding edge cases that share the same code path.                                   |
   346→| **feature**  | Integration tests             | Verify end-to-end flows through the new capability. Cover happy path, key error paths, and permission boundaries.                     |
   347→| **refactor** | Regression tests              | Existing tests should continue to pass unchanged. If coverage gaps exist around the refactored area, add unit tests before starting.  |
   348→| **debt**     | Compatibility tests           | Verify that upgraded dependencies or migrated configs still produce identical behaviour in CI.                                        |
   349→| **docs**     | Review checklist              | Docs should be technically accurate (code samples run), internally consistent, and pass any linter or link-checker in the project.    |
   350→| **perf**     | Performance / benchmark tests | Before-and-after benchmark under controlled conditions. Record environment, dataset, and iteration count so results are reproducible. |
   351→
   352→Write the `## Test Guidance` section using this structure:
   353→
   354→```markdown
   355→## Test Guidance
   356→
   357→**Primary category:** <category from table>
   358→
   359→**What to test:**
   360→
   361→- <specific behaviour or scenario 1>
   362→- <specific behaviour or scenario 2>
   363→
   364→**Boundary conditions:**
   365→
   366→- <edge case worth covering>
   367→
   368→**Infrastructure needs:**
   369→
   370→- <any fixtures, mocks, environments, or data required>
   371→```
   372→
   373→---
   374→
   375→## 6 — Definition of Done
   376→
   377→Every issue includes a `## Definition of Done` section. Start with a shared
   378→baseline, then append type-specific gates.
   379→
   380→### Shared baseline (always include)
   381→
   382→```markdown
   383→## Definition of Done
   384→
   385→- [ ] Implementation is complete and builds without errors.
   386→- [ ] All new and existing tests pass in CI.
   387→- [ ] Code has been reviewed and approved by at least one other engineer.
   388→- [ ] Changes are documented — at minimum, meaningful commit messages and
   389→      inline comments where intent is non-obvious.
   390→```
   391→
   392→### Type-specific gates
   393→
   394→Append the relevant extras below the baseline checklist:
   395→
   396→**bug**
   397→
   398→```markdown
   399→- [ ] A test that reproduces the original bug is included and passes.
   400→- [ ] Regression has been verified in an environment matching the reporter's setup.
   401→```
   402→
   403→**feature**
   404→
   405→```markdown
   406→- [ ] All success criteria listed in the issue are met.
   407→- [ ] User-facing documentation or changelog entry is updated.
   408→- [ ] Feature flag or rollout strategy is in place (if applicable).
   409→```
   410→
   411→**refactor**
   412→
   413→```markdown
   414→- [ ] All behavioural invariants listed in the issue still hold.
   415→- [ ] No new warnings or linter violations introduced.
   416→```
   417→
   418→**debt**
   419→
   420→```markdown
   421→- [ ] The targeted debt item is fully resolved (dependency upgraded, deprecation
   422→      removed, etc.).
   423→- [ ] Rollback path has been tested or documented.
   424→```
   425→
   426→**docs**
   427→
   428→```markdown
   429→- [ ] Documentation has been reviewed for technical accuracy by a domain owner.
   430→- [ ] All code samples have been tested and run successfully.
   431→- [ ] Links are valid and not broken.
   432→```
   433→
   434→**perf**
   435→
   436→```markdown
   437→- [ ] Performance targets defined in the issue are met or exceeded.
   438→- [ ] Benchmark results are recorded and attached to the issue or PR.
   439→- [ ] No functional regressions introduced by the optimisation.
   440→```
   441→
   442→---
   443→
   444→## 7 — Generation workflow (step by step)
   445→
   446→When asked to create an issue, follow these steps in order:
   447→
   448→1. **Parse the task description.** Extract the core intent, affected components,
   449→   and any constraints the user mentioned.
   450→2. **Classify the type** using the cue table and disambiguation rules in §3.
   451→3. **Assess scope** using the heuristics in §2. Decide: single issue or split.
   452→4. **Select the template** from §4 matching the classified type.
   453→5. **Fill in every section.** Do not leave placeholders like "TBD" — if information
   454→   is missing, state what is unknown and what assumptions you are making.
   455→6. **Write the Test Guidance** section per §5.
   456→7. **Write the Definition of Done** section per §6.
   457→8. **Name the file** per the conventions in §1 and write it to `.issues/`.
   458→9. **If splitting**, create the parent first, then each child. Ensure cross-links
   459→   are correct.
   460→10. **Present the file(s)** to the user for review.
   461→
   462→---
   463→
   464→## 8 — Full single-issue example
   465→
   466→Below is a condensed example to show how the pieces fit together. Real issues
   467→should be more thorough; this is for structural illustration.
   468→
   469→```markdown
   470→# CSV export crashes on empty filter result
   471→
   472→| Field        | Value      |
   473→| ------------ | ---------- |
   474→| **Type**     | `bug`      |
   475→| **Priority** | `p1`       |
   476→| **Parent**   | —          |
   477→| **Created**  | 2026-03-12 |
   478→
   479→## Summary
   480→
   481→Exporting a filtered dataset to CSV throws an unhandled `IndexError` when the
   482→filter returns zero rows. Users see a 500 page instead of an empty file.
   483→
   484→## Environment
   485→
   486→- Python 3.12, Django 5.1, Pandas 2.2
   487→- Reproduced on staging (Linux/Docker) and locally (macOS 15).
   488→
   489→## Steps to Reproduce
   490→
   491→1. Navigate to /reports and apply a date filter for a range with no data.
   492→2. Click "Export CSV".
   493→3. Observe the 500 error.
   494→
   495→## Expected Behaviour
   496→
   497→The server responds with a valid CSV file containing only the header row.
   498→
   499→## Actual Behaviour
   500→
   501→`IndexError: single positional indexer is out-of-bounds` in
   502→`export_service.py:42`. Full traceback is in the attached log.
   503→
   504→## Root Cause Analysis
   505→
   506→`export_csv()` calls `df.iloc[0]` to sniff column types before writing.
   507→When the dataframe is empty this raises `IndexError`.
   508→
   509→## Proposed Fix
   510→
   511→Guard with an `if df.empty` check and short-circuit to writing headers only.
   512→
   513→## Test Guidance
   514→
   515→**Primary category:** Unit tests
   516→
   517→**What to test:**
   518→
   519→- Exporting with zero rows returns a CSV containing only the header.
   520→- Exporting with one row and many rows still works as before.
   521→
   522→**Boundary conditions:**
   523→
   524→- DataFrame with columns but zero rows.
   525→- DataFrame with zero columns (unlikely but defensive).
   526→
   527→**Infrastructure needs:**
   528→
   529→- Existing `ReportFactory` test fixture generates sample data; extend it to
   530→  produce an empty-result filter scenario.
   531→
   532→## Definition of Done
   533→
   534→- [ ] Implementation is complete and builds without errors.
   535→- [ ] All new and existing tests pass in CI.
   536→- [ ] Code has been reviewed and approved by at least one other engineer.
   537→- [ ] Changes are documented — at minimum, meaningful commit messages and
   538→      inline comments where intent is non-obvious.
   539→- [ ] A test that reproduces the original bug is included and passes.
   540→- [ ] Regression has been verified in an environment matching the reporter's
   541→      setup.
   542→```
   543→
   544→---
   545→
   546→## 9 — Full split-issue example (parent)
   547→
   548→```markdown
   549→# Payment flow redesign
   550→
   551→| Field        | Value      |
   552→| ------------ | ---------- |
   553→| **Type**     | `feature`  |
   554→| **Priority** | `p1`       |
   555→| **Parent**   | —          |
   556→| **Created**  | 2026-03-12 |
   557→
   558→## Summary
   559→
   560→Replace the legacy single-step payment page with a multi-step checkout flow
   561→that supports saved payment methods and real-time validation.
   562→
   563→## Child Issues
   564→
   565→- [ ] [API design](feature-payment-flow--api-design.md)
   566→- [ ] [UI multi-step form](feature-payment-flow--ui-form.md)
   567→- [ ] [Fix rounding error in tax calc](bug-payment-flow--rounding-error.md)
   568→
   569→## User Story
   570→
   571→As a customer, I want a guided checkout flow so that I can complete purchases
   572→with fewer errors and the option to reuse saved cards.
   573→
   574→## Success Criteria
   575→
   576→- [ ] Checkout converts at least as well as the current single-step page.
   577→- [ ] Saved cards are usable without re-entering details.
   578→- [ ] Tax is calculated correctly to the cent for all supported locales.
   579→
   580→## Out of Scope
   581→
   582→- Subscription / recurring billing (tracked separately).
   583→- Gift card support.
   584→```
   585→
   586→Each child carries its own full template, Test Guidance, and Definition of Done.
   587→

file.content:
---
name: issue_writer
description: >
  Generate structured, type-aware issue markdown files from task descriptions.
  Use this skill whenever the user asks to create issues, tickets, tasks, bug reports,
  feature requests, or any project-tracking artifact written as markdown. Also trigger
  when the user says things like "write me an issue for…", "break this work down",
  "I need to track this task", "document this bug", "create a ticket", or describes
  work that needs scoping, splitting, or formal specification. The skill covers scope
  assessment, issue-type classification, template selection, test guidance, and
  Definition of Done — and writes all output into a `.issues/` directory at the
  project root.
---

# Issue Writer

Write comprehensive, agent-readable issue markdown files from raw task descriptions.
Every issue is self-contained: an agent (or human) picking it up should understand
the problem, the shape of the solution, how to verify it, and when it is done —
without reading anything else.

---

## 1 — Output location and naming

Store every generated issue file inside a `.issues/` directory at the **root** of the
project (create it if it does not exist).

### Naming convention

```
.issues/<type>-<slug>.md
```

- `<type>` is one of: `bug`, `feature`, `refactor`, `debt`, `docs`, `perf`.
- `<slug>` is a lowercase, hyphen-separated summary (3–6 words max).

**Examples:**

```
.issues/bug-null-pointer-on-empty-input.md
.issues/feature-user-export-csv.md
.issues/refactor-extract-auth-middleware.md
```

When a task is split into a parent with children, create:

```
.issues/feature-payment-flow.md              ← parent
.issues/feature-payment-flow--api-design.md  ← child
.issues/feature-payment-flow--ui-form.md     ← child
.issues/bug-payment-flow--rounding-error.md  ← child (different type is OK)
```

The double-hyphen `--` separates the parent slug from the child slug. Children
reference the parent file path in their front matter.

---

## 2 — Scope assessment: single issue or split?

Before writing anything, evaluate scope. The goal is that each issue represents a
single reviewable, testable, deployable unit of work.

### Heuristics for splitting

Consider splitting when **two or more** of these are true:

| Signal                        | Indicator                                                              |
| ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Distinct concerns**         | The task touches unrelated subsystems (e.g., API + UI + DB migration). |
| **Multiple actors**           | Different people or teams would naturally own different parts.         |
| **Independent deployability** | Parts could ship and deliver value on their own.                       |
| **Estimated effort**          | Total effort exceeds roughly 1–2 working days for one person.          |
| **Dependency chain**          | One part blocks another — explicit ordering helps.                     |
| **Mixed types**               | The task blends a bug fix with a new feature or refactoring.           |

If only zero or one signal fires, keep it as a single issue.

### When you split

1. Create a **parent issue** that describes the overall objective and lists every
   child with its file path.
2. Create one **child issue per concern**. Each child carries its own type, template,
   tests, and Definition of Done.
3. In the parent, include a section called `## Child Issues` with a checklist:
   ```markdown
   ## Child Issues

   - [ ] [API design](feature-payment-flow--api-design.md)
   - [ ] [UI form](feature-payment-flow--ui-form.md)
   - [ ] [Fix rounding](bug-payment-flow--rounding-error.md)
   ```
4. In each child, add front-matter or a header line:
   ```markdown
   **Parent:** [Payment Flow](feature-payment-flow.md)
   ```

---

## 3 — Classifying the issue type

Determine the type from the task description before selecting a template. This
table maps common language cues to issue types:

| Type         | Typical cues in the description                                                                                         |
| ------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **bug**      | "broken", "crash", "incorrect output", "regression", "doesn't work", error messages, stack traces, unexpected behaviour |
| **feature**  | "add", "new capability", "user should be able to", "support for", "as a user I want"                                    |
| **refactor** | "clean up", "restructure", "extract", "rename", "simplify", "reduce coupling" — no observable behaviour change          |
| **debt**     | "upgrade dependency", "remove deprecated API", "fix linter warnings", "address TODOs", "migrate to new library"         |
| **docs**     | "document", "README", "API reference", "add examples", "onboarding guide"                                               |
| **perf**     | "slow", "timeout", "optimise", "reduce memory", "latency", benchmark numbers                                            |

### Disambiguation rules for ambiguous tasks

Some tasks straddle types. Apply these tie-breakers in order:

1. **Behaviour is currently wrong** → `bug`, even if the fix also improves code
   structure.
2. **External behaviour changes for the user** → `feature`, even if it also
   cleans things up internally.
3. **Performance is the primary goal** → `perf`, even if the approach is
   restructuring code.
4. **No external behaviour changes at all** → `refactor` if the code is being
   reshaped, `debt` if the change is about maintenance hygiene (dependency
   bumps, warning suppression, config migration).
5. **The deliverable is prose, not code** → `docs`.

If genuinely uncertain after these steps, pick the type whose template captures
the most useful information for the task and note the ambiguity in the issue body
under a `> **Classification note:**` blockquote.

---

## 4 — Templates by issue type

Every issue begins with a common header, then diverges by type.

### 4.0 — Common header (all types)

```markdown
# <Title>

| Field        | Value         |
| ------------ | ------------- |
| **Type**     | `<type>`      |
| **Priority** | `<p0–p3>`     |
| **Parent**   | (path or "—") |
| **Created**  | <YYYY-MM-DD>  |

## Summary

A 1–3 sentence plain-language description of what this issue is about and why it
matters.
```

Priority levels:

- **p0** — System down or data loss; drop everything.
- **p1** — Significant impact; address within the current sprint.
- **p2** — Important but not urgent; schedule soon.
- **p3** — Nice-to-have; backlog.

---

### 4.1 — Bug

```markdown
## Environment

Runtime, OS, language version, relevant dependency versions.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. …
2. …
3. …

## Expected Behaviour

What should happen.

## Actual Behaviour

What happens instead. Include error messages, logs, or screenshots if available.

## Root Cause Analysis

If the cause is known or suspected, describe it here. Otherwise write
"To be investigated."

## Proposed Fix

High-level approach to the fix (not a full implementation plan).
```

---

### 4.2 — Feature

```markdown
## User Story

As a <role>, I want <capability>, so that <benefit>.

## Detailed Description

Expand on the user story: context, workflows affected, edge cases to consider.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Criterion 1 — observable, verifiable outcome.
- [ ] Criterion 2
- …

## Design Considerations

API contracts, UI wireframe notes, data model changes, or architectural
decisions. Link to external design docs if they exist.

## Out of Scope

Explicitly list things this issue does NOT cover to prevent scope creep.
```

---

### 4.3 — Refactor

```markdown
## Current State

Describe the code or architecture as it exists today and why it is problematic.

## Target State

Describe the desired structure after the refactor.

## Migration Strategy

Step-by-step approach to move from current to target without breaking things.
Favour incremental steps that keep the build green.

## Behavioural Invariants

List the external behaviours that must remain unchanged after this refactor.
These become your regression-test anchors.
```

---

### 4.4 — Technical Debt

```markdown
## Debt Description

What the debt is and how it accumulated.

## Impact

Concrete consequences of leaving this debt in place (slower builds, security
exposure, blocked upgrades, developer friction).

## Remediation Plan

Ordered steps to pay down the debt.

## Rollback Considerations

What to do if the remediation causes unexpected problems.
```

---

### 4.5 — Documentation

```markdown
## Audience

Who will read this documentation (end-users, developers, ops, etc.).

## Scope

Which topics or APIs the documentation covers.

## Outline

Proposed structure / table of contents for the documentation deliverable.

## Source Material

Links to code, existing docs, conversations, or specs that inform the content.
```

---

### 4.6 — Performance

```markdown
## Current Performance

Measurements or observations: latency, throughput, memory, CPU, bundle size.
Include how measurements were taken (tool, environment, dataset).

## Target Performance

Quantitative targets that define success (e.g., "p99 latency < 200ms under 1k
concurrent requests").

## Hypothesis

What you believe is causing the bottleneck and why.

## Proposed Approach

Optimisation strategy — algorithmic change, caching, parallelism, resource
pooling, etc.

## Benchmark Plan

How performance will be measured before and after to validate improvement.
```

---

## 5 — Test definition

Every issue includes a `## Test Guidance` section. The content varies by type —
the aim is to tell the implementer _what kinds_ of tests to write, not to supply
code.

### Principles

- Describe the **category** of test and what it should exercise, not the
  implementation.
- Be specific about **boundary conditions** and **critical paths** worth testing.
- Mention any infrastructure the tests will need (fixtures, mocks, staging
  environment, seed data).

### Type-specific guidance

| Type         | Primary test category         | Focus areas                                                                                                                           |
| ------------ | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **bug**      | Unit tests                    | Reproduce the exact failing case first. Cover surrounding edge cases that share the same code path.                                   |
| **feature**  | Integration tests             | Verify end-to-end flows through the new capability. Cover happy path, key error paths, and permission boundaries.                     |
| **refactor** | Regression tests              | Existing tests should continue to pass unchanged. If coverage gaps exist around the refactored area, add unit tests before starting.  |
| **debt**     | Compatibility tests           | Verify that upgraded dependencies or migrated configs still produce identical behaviour in CI.                                        |
| **docs**     | Review checklist              | Docs should be technically accurate (code samples run), internally consistent, and pass any linter or link-checker in the project.    |
| **perf**     | Performance / benchmark tests | Before-and-after benchmark under controlled conditions. Record environment, dataset, and iteration count so results are reproducible. |

Write the `## Test Guidance` section using this structure:

```markdown
## Test Guidance

**Primary category:** <category from table>

**What to test:**

- <specific behaviour or scenario 1>
- <specific behaviour or scenario 2>

**Boundary conditions:**

- <edge case worth covering>

**Infrastructure needs:**

- <any fixtures, mocks, environments, or data required>
```

---

## 6 — Definition of Done

Every issue includes a `## Definition of Done` section. Start with a shared
baseline, then append type-specific gates.

### Shared baseline (always include)

```markdown
## Definition of Done

- [ ] Implementation is complete and builds without errors.
- [ ] All new and existing tests pass in CI.
- [ ] Code has been reviewed and approved by at least one other engineer.
- [ ] Changes are documented — at minimum, meaningful commit messages and
      inline comments where intent is non-obvious.
```

### Type-specific gates

Append the relevant extras below the baseline checklist:

**bug**

```markdown
- [ ] A test that reproduces the original bug is included and passes.
- [ ] Regression has been verified in an environment matching the reporter's setup.
```

**feature**

```markdown
- [ ] All success criteria listed in the issue are met.
- [ ] User-facing documentation or changelog entry is updated.
- [ ] Feature flag or rollout strategy is in place (if applicable).
```

**refactor**

```markdown
- [ ] All behavioural invariants listed in the issue still hold.
- [ ] No new warnings or linter violations introduced.
```

**debt**

```markdown
- [ ] The targeted debt item is fully resolved (dependency upgraded, deprecation
      removed, etc.).
- [ ] Rollback path has been tested or documented.
```

**docs**

```markdown
- [ ] Documentation has been reviewed for technical accuracy by a domain owner.
- [ ] All code samples have been tested and run successfully.
- [ ] Links are valid and not broken.
```

**perf**

```markdown
- [ ] Performance targets defined in the issue are met or exceeded.
- [ ] Benchmark results are recorded and attached to the issue or PR.
- [ ] No functional regressions introduced by the optimisation.
```

---

## 7 — Generation workflow (step by step)

When asked to create an issue, follow these steps in order:

1. **Parse the task description.** Extract the core intent, affected components,
   and any constraints the user mentioned.
2. **Classify the type** using the cue table and disambiguation rules in §3.
3. **Assess scope** using the heuristics in §2. Decide: single issue or split.
4. **Select the template** from §4 matching the classified type.
5. **Fill in every section.** Do not leave placeholders like "TBD" — if information
   is missing, state what is unknown and what assumptions you are making.
6. **Write the Test Guidance** section per §5.
7. **Write the Definition of Done** section per §6.
8. **Name the file** per the conventions in §1 and write it to `.issues/`.
9. **If splitting**, create the parent first, then each child. Ensure cross-links
   are correct.
10. **Present the file(s)** to the user for review.

---

## 8 — Full single-issue example

Below is a condensed example to show how the pieces fit together. Real issues
should be more thorough; this is for structural illustration.

```markdown
# CSV export crashes on empty filter result

| Field        | Value      |
| ------------ | ---------- |
| **Type**     | `bug`      |
| **Priority** | `p1`       |
| **Parent**   | —          |
| **Created**  | 2026-03-12 |

## Summary

Exporting a filtered dataset to CSV throws an unhandled `IndexError` when the
filter returns zero rows. Users see a 500 page instead of an empty file.

## Environment

- Python 3.12, Django 5.1, Pandas 2.2
- Reproduced on staging (Linux/Docker) and locally (macOS 15).

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Navigate to /reports and apply a date filter for a range with no data.
2. Click "Export CSV".
3. Observe the 500 error.

## Expected Behaviour

The server responds with a valid CSV file containing only the header row.

## Actual Behaviour

`IndexError: single positional indexer is out-of-bounds` in
`export_service.py:42`. Full traceback is in the attached log.

## Root Cause Analysis

`export_csv()` calls `df.iloc[0]` to sniff column types before writing.
When the dataframe is empty this raises `IndexError`.

## Proposed Fix

Guard with an `if df.empty` check and short-circuit to writing headers only.

## Test Guidance

**Primary category:** Unit tests

**What to test:**

- Exporting with zero rows returns a CSV containing only the header.
- Exporting with one row and many rows still works as before.

**Boundary conditions:**

- DataFrame with columns but zero rows.
- DataFrame with zero columns (unlikely but defensive).

**Infrastructure needs:**

- Existing `ReportFactory` test fixture generates sample data; extend it to
  produce an empty-result filter scenario.

## Definition of Done

- [ ] Implementation is complete and builds without errors.
- [ ] All new and existing tests pass in CI.
- [ ] Code has been reviewed and approved by at least one other engineer.
- [ ] Changes are documented — at minimum, meaningful commit messages and
      inline comments where intent is non-obvious.
- [ ] A test that reproduces the original bug is included and passes.
- [ ] Regression has been verified in an environment matching the reporter's
      setup.
```

---

## 9 — Full split-issue example (parent)

```markdown
# Payment flow redesign

| Field        | Value      |
| ------------ | ---------- |
| **Type**     | `feature`  |
| **Priority** | `p1`       |
| **Parent**   | —          |
| **Created**  | 2026-03-12 |

## Summary

Replace the legacy single-step payment page with a multi-step checkout flow
that supports saved payment methods and real-time validation.

## Child Issues

- [ ] [API design](feature-payment-flow--api-design.md)
- [ ] [UI multi-step form](feature-payment-flow--ui-form.md)
- [ ] [Fix rounding error in tax calc](bug-payment-flow--rounding-error.md)

## User Story

As a customer, I want a guided checkout flow so that I can complete purchases
with fewer errors and the option to reuse saved cards.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Checkout converts at least as well as the current single-step page.
- [ ] Saved cards are usable without re-entering details.
- [ ] Tax is calculated correctly to the cent for all supported locales.

## Out of Scope

- Subscription / recurring billing (tracked separately).
- Gift card support.
```

Each child carries its own full template, Test Guidance, and Definition of Done.

content.content:
1→# Zed Git Crates: Deep Architecture Analysis
     2→### `crates/git` · `crates/project` (GitStore) · `crates/buffer_diff`
     3→
     4→> **Purpose of this document:** A thorough implementation reference for the three supporting crates that underpin the Git Panel. It covers every major type, trait, data structure, and design decision, with rationale and Rust code skeletons oriented toward replicating the pattern in a private GPUI project.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## How the Four Crates Relate
     9→
    10→```
    11→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    12→│  crates/git_ui   (GitPanel, ProjectDiff, ConflictView)       │
    13→│  → reads Entity<GitStore> for all state                      │
    14→│  → calls repo.update(cx, |r,cx| r.stage_entries(..))        │
    15→└─────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
    16→                              │ Entity<GitStore>
    17→┌─────────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────┐
    18→│  crates/project  (GitStore, Repository, BufferDiffStore)     │
    19→│  → owns HashMap<RepositoryId, Entity<Repository>>           │
    20→│  → creates/destroys Repository entities on worktree events  │
    21→│  → owns HashMap<BufferId, (Entity<BufferDiff>,...)>          │
    22→└───────────────┬─────────────────────────┬───────────────────┘
    23→                │ Arc<dyn GitRepository>   │ Entity<Buffer>
    24→┌───────────────▼──────────┐  ┌───────────▼───────────────────┐
    25→│  crates/git              │  │  crates/buffer_diff            │
    26→│  GitRepository trait     │  │  BufferDiff  (Entity<T>)       │
    27→│  RealGitRepository (git2)│  │  BufferDiffSnapshot (immutable)│
    28→│  FakeGitRepository (test)│  │  DiffHunk, SecondaryHunkStatus │
    29→│  StatusEntry, BlameEntry │  │  imara-diff algorithm          │
    30→└──────────────────────────┘  └───────────────────────────────┘
    31→```
    32→
    33→The dependency graph is strictly layered:
    34→
    35→- `git` knows nothing about GPUI, Project, or the UI.
    36→- `buffer_diff` knows about `language::Buffer` but not about `git` ops.
    37→- `project/git_store` assembles both: it holds `Arc<dyn GitRepository>` for
    38→  pure git operations and `Entity<BufferDiff>` for each open buffer's diff.
    39→- `git_ui` only talks to `GitStore` and `Repository` through GPUI entities
    40→  — it never calls `git2` directly.
    41→
    42→This layering is the foundational design decision that makes the whole
    43→system testable, remotable, and replaceable.
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## Part 1 — `crates/git`: The Pure Git Abstraction Layer
    48→
    49→### 1.1  What this crate is
    50→
    51→`crates/git` is a **thin, synchronous, trait-based wrapper** around both
    52→the `git2` Rust bindings to libgit2 and the `git` CLI binary (used for
    53→operations libgit2 can't do, such as complex push/pull authentication
    54→flows). It has no async runtime, no GPUI types, and no side effects beyond
    55→touching the filesystem/index.
    56→
    57→The crate's entire surface is the `GitRepository` trait. Everything else —
    58→status entries, blame entries, branch info — is a data type.
    59→
    60→### 1.2  The `GitRepository` Trait (crates/git/src/repository.rs)
    61→
    62→```rust
    63→pub trait GitRepository: Send + Sync {
    64→    // ── Identity ───────────────────────────────────────────
    65→    fn path(&self) -> &Path;               // .git directory path
    66→    fn work_directory(&self) -> Option<&Path>;
    67→
    68→    // ── Staging / Index ────────────────────────────────────
    69→    fn stage_paths(
    70→        &self,
    71→        paths: &[RepoPath],
    72→        env: &HashMap<String, String>,
    73→    ) -> Result<()>;
    74→
    75→    fn unstage_paths(
    76→        &self,
    77→        paths: &[RepoPath],
    78→        env: &HashMap<String, String>,
    79→    ) -> Result<()>;
    80→
    81→    /// Write arbitrary text directly to the git index for one path.
    82→    /// This is how hunk-level staging works: the caller computes
    83→    /// the desired index content and writes it, bypassing `git add`.
    84→    fn set_index_text(
    85→        &self,
    86→        path: &RepoPath,
    87→        content: Option<String>,    // None = remove from index
    88→        env: &HashMap<String, String>,
    89→    ) -> Result<()>;
    90→
    91→    fn reload_index(&self);
    92→
    93→    // ── Commit ─────────────────────────────────────────────
    94→    fn commit(
    95→        &self,
    96→        message: &str,
    97→        options: &CommitOptions,
    98→        env: &HashMap<String, String>,
    99→    ) -> Result<()>;
   100→
   101→    fn uncommit(&self, env: &HashMap<String, String>) -> Result<()>;
   102→    // uncommit = git reset HEAD^ --soft
   103→
   104→    // ── Remote operations ──────────────────────────────────
   105→    fn push(
   106→        &self,
   107→        branch: &str,
   108→        remote: Option<&str>,
   109→        options: &PushOptions,
   110→        env: &HashMap<String, String>,
   111→    ) -> Result<()>;
   112→
   113→    fn pull(
   114→        &self,
   115→        rebase: bool,
   116→        env: &HashMap<String, String>,
   117→    ) -> Result<()>;
   118→
   119→    fn fetch(&self, env: &HashMap<String, String>) -> Result<()>;
   120→
   121→    fn create_remote(&self, name: &str, url: &str) -> Result<()>;
   122→
   123→    // ── Status ─────────────────────────────────────────────
   124→    fn status(&self, path_prefixes: &[RepoPath]) -> Result<Vec<StatusEntry>>;
   125→    fn status_for_path(&self, path: &RepoPath) -> Result<Option<StatusEntry>>;
   126→
   127→    // ── Branch operations ──────────────────────────────────
   128→    fn current_branch(&self) -> Option<Branch>;
   129→    fn branches(&self) -> Result<Vec<Branch>>;
   130→    fn create_branch(&self, name: &str) -> Result<()>;
   131→    fn checkout(&self, target: &str, env: &HashMap<String, String>) -> Result<()>;
   132→    fn delete_branch(&self, name: &str) -> Result<()>;
   133→    fn merge_base(&self, a: &str, b: &str) -> Result<Option<String>>;
   134→    fn remote_url(&self, name: &str) -> Option<String>;
   135→
   136→    // ── Diff / Content ─────────────────────────────────────
   137→    /// Read a file from HEAD (for diff base text).
   138→    fn head_text_for_path(&self, path: &RepoPath) -> Result<Option<String>>;
   139→
   140→    /// Read a file from the index (for staging status diff base).
   141→    fn index_text_for_path(&self, path: &RepoPath) -> Result<Option<String>>;
   142→
   143→    // ── Blame ──────────────────────────────────────────────
   144→    fn blame_for_path(
   145→        &self,
   146→        path: &RepoPath,
   147→        content: Rope,
   148→    ) -> Result<Vec<BlameEntry>>;
   149→
   150→    // ── Stash ──────────────────────────────────────────────
   151→    fn stash_list(&self) -> Result<Vec<StashEntry>>;
   152→    fn stash_all(&self, message: Option<&str>) -> Result<()>;
   153→    fn stash_pop(&self, index: usize) -> Result<()>;
   154→    fn stash_apply(&self, index: usize) -> Result<()>;
   155→    fn stash_drop(&self, index: usize) -> Result<()>;
   156→
   157→    // ── History ────────────────────────────────────────────
   158→    fn log(&self, path: Option<&RepoPath>, limit: usize) -> Result<Vec<CommitSummary>>;
   159→    fn show(&self, oid: &str) -> Result<CommitDetails>;
   160→
   161→    // ── Conflict resolution ────────────────────────────────
   162→    fn checkout_conflict_path(&self, path: &RepoPath, side: ConflictSide) -> Result<()>;
   163→}
   164→```
   165→
   166→**Key design decisions:**
   167→
   168→1. **All methods are synchronous.** The async wrapper is in `project/git_store.rs`, not here. This makes the trait trivially testable.
   169→2. **`env: &HashMap<String, String>` parameter.** Every mutating operation receives a git environment map. This is how the AskPass credential helper is injected — the map contains `GIT_ASKPASS=/path/to/helper` and a unique delegate ID. The implementation forwards this into the git2 process environment or into the CLI call.
   170→3. **Dual backend.** Pure metadata operations (status, blame, log, index text) use the `git2` library for speed; network operations (push, pull, fetch) shell out to the `git` CLI because libgit2's HTTP/SSH authentication does not support the same credential helper ecosystem as the CLI.
   171→
   172→### 1.3  Data Types
   173→
   174→#### `RepoPath`
   175→
   176→```rust
   177→/// A path relative to the repository root (not the worktree root).
   178→/// Implements `Ord` for use in `SumTree`.
   179→pub struct RepoPath(Arc<Path>);
   180→```
   181→
   182→`RepoPath` is the canonical identifier for a file within a repository. It is **not** a `ProjectPath` (which includes a `WorktreeId`) and **not** an absolute path. The conversion between them is handled in `git_store.rs`.
   183→
   184→#### `StatusEntry`
   185→
   186→```rust
   187→pub struct StatusEntry {
   188→    pub repo_path: RepoPath,
   189→    pub status: GitFileStatus,
   190→}
   191→
   192→pub struct GitFileStatus {
   193→    pub index_status: FileStatus,     // staged state (vs HEAD)
   194→    pub worktree_status: FileStatus,  // unstaged state (vs index)
   195→    pub conflict: bool,               // unmerged paths
   196→}
   197→
   198→pub enum FileStatus {
   199→    Added,
   200→    Modified,

content.text: Let me read more of the epic file and check the project structure.

content.content:
200→    Modified,
   201→    Deleted,
   202→    Untracked,
   203→    Unchanged,
   204→}
   205→```
   206→
   207→`StatusEntry` is the atomic unit of git status. A single file entry encodes **both** its staged and unstaged state simultaneously — this is what enables the three-state checkbox in the UI without needing two separate lists.
   208→
   209→The staging state of a file is derived by comparing `index_status` and `worktree_status`:
   210→
   211→```
   212→index_status == Unchanged && worktree_status != Unchanged  → fully unstaged
   213→index_status != Unchanged && worktree_status == Unchanged  → fully staged
   214→both non-Unchanged                                          → partially staged
   215→```
   216→
   217→#### `BlameEntry`
   218→
   219→```rust
   220→pub struct BlameEntry {
   221→    pub sha: Oid,               // commit SHA
   222→    pub line_range: Range<u32>, // 0-based line range this commit owns
   223→    pub author: Option<String>,
   224→    pub author_mail: Option<String>,
   225→    pub author_time: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
   226→    pub committer: Option<String>,
   227→    pub summary: Option<String>,
   228→}
   229→```
   230→
   231→Blame entries are stored in a `SumTree<BlameEntry>` keyed by line range, enabling O(log N) lookup of "which commit owns line N".
   232→
   233→#### `Branch`
   234→
   235→```rust
   236→pub struct Branch {
   237→    pub name: SharedString,
   238→    pub upstream: Option<UpstreamBranch>,
   239→    pub is_head: bool,
   240→    pub unix_timestamp: Option<i64>,  // for sorting by recency
   241→}
   242→
   243→pub struct UpstreamBranch {
   244→    pub name: SharedString,
   245→    pub ahead: Option<u32>,
   246→    pub behind: Option<u32>,
   247→}
   248→```
   249→
   250→Ahead/behind counts power the push/pull badge displayed in the GitPanel toolbar.
   251→
   252→#### `CommitOptions`
   253→
   254→```rust
   255→pub struct CommitOptions {
   256→    pub amend: bool,
   257→    pub signoff: bool,
   258→}
   259→```
   260→
   261→Passed directly to `GitRepository::commit()`. The UI toggle maps directly to these fields.
   262→
   263→### 1.4  `RealGitRepository` — the git2 implementation
   264→
   265→```rust
   266→pub struct RealGitRepository {
   267→    // The libgit2 repository handle — NOT Send by default;
   268→    // we wrap it in a Mutex to allow use from background threads.
   269→    repository: Mutex<git2::Repository>,
   270→    // Cached path so we don't lock for read-only queries.
   271→    path: PathBuf,
   272→    work_directory: Option<PathBuf>,
   273→}
   274→```
   275→
   276→**Critical implementation note:** `git2::Repository` is not `Sync`, so Zed
   277→wraps it in a `Mutex`. The background job worker (in `git_store.rs`) holds
   278→the `Arc<dyn GitRepository>` and the `Mutex` is locked for the duration of
   279→each operation. This is safe because all git operations are serialized
   280→through the job queue anyway.
   281→
   282→**Status reading** uses `git2::Repository::statuses()` with
   283→`StatusOptions::include_untracked(true)` and maps the `git2::Status` bitmask
   284→into Zed's `GitFileStatus` struct.
   285→
   286→**Index writes** for `set_index_text()` use `git2::Repository::index()` to get
   287→the in-memory index, then:
   288→
   289→```rust
   290→// For set_index_text(path, Some(content)):
   291→let oid = repo.blob(content.as_bytes())?;
   292→let entry = IndexEntry { path: ..., id: oid, mode: 0o100644, ... };
   293→index.add(&entry)?;
   294→index.write()?;
   295→
   296→// For set_index_text(path, None):
   297→index.remove_path(path)?;
   298→index.write()?;
   299→```
   300→
   301→After writing, `reload_index()` is called, which calls `index.read(true)` to
   302→reload from disk (necessary because other tools may write the index).
   303→
   304→**Push/pull/fetch** are implemented by spawning a `git` process:
   305→
   306→```rust
   307→Command::new("git")
   308→    .args(["push", remote, branch])
   309→    .envs(env)          // includes GIT_ASKPASS
   310→    .current_dir(&self.work_directory)
   311→    .spawn()?
   312→```
   313→
   314→The `env` map passed from `git_store.rs` contains at minimum:
   315→- `GIT_ASKPASS` — path to the Zed askpass helper binary
   316→- `ZEDASKPASS_DELEGATE_ID` — unique integer identifying which `AskPassDelegate` in `GitStore` receives the credential prompt
   317→
   318→### 1.5  `FakeGitRepository` — the test double
   319→
   320→```rust
   321→pub struct FakeGitRepository {
   322→    // Head commit: path → content
   323→    head: HashMap<RepoPath, String>,
   324→    // Index: path → content (staged state)
   325→    index: HashMap<RepoPath, String>,
   326→    // Working tree: path → content (read from FakeFs)
   327→    fs: Arc<FakeFs>,
   328→    work_directory: PathBuf,
   329→}
   330→```
   331→
   332→`FakeGitRepository` derives status by comparing `head`, `index`, and `fs`
   333→contents — it does not call any git binary. After the PR
   334→[#26961](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/pull/26961) it generates
   335→realistic status: if you modify a file in `FakeFs`, `status()` returns
   336→`Modified`. This allows integration tests to exercise the full
   337→`GitStore → Repository → Panel` reactive chain without a real git repository.
   338→
   339→To use it in tests:
   340→
   341→```rust
   342→let repo = FakeGitRepository::new(fs.clone(), root_path);
   343→repo.set_head_content(RepoPath::from("src/main.rs"), "fn main() {}");
   344→// Modify the file in the fake filesystem...
   345→fs.set_content(root.join("src/main.rs"), "fn main() { println!(\"hi\"); }").await;
   346→// Now repo.status() will return Modified for src/main.rs
   347→```
   348→
   349→### 1.6  `crates/git/src/status.rs` — SumTree integration
   350→
   351→`StatusEntry` implements the `sum_tree::Item` trait, which requires a
   352→`Summary` type and a `key()` method:
   353→
   354→```rust
   355→impl sum_tree::Item for StatusEntry {
   356→    type Summary = PathSummary;
   357→    fn summary(&self) -> Self::Summary {
   358→        PathSummary {
   359→            max_path: self.repo_path.clone(),
   360→            count: 1,
   361→        }
   362→    }
   363→}
   364→
   365→#[derive(Clone, Default)]
   366→pub struct PathSummary {
   367→    pub max_path: RepoPath,
   368→    pub count: usize,
   369→}
   370→
   371→impl sum_tree::Summary for PathSummary {
   372→    type Context = ();
   373→    fn add_summary(&mut self, other: &Self, _: &()) {
   374→        if other.max_path > self.max_path {
   375→            self.max_path = other.max_path.clone();
   376→        }
   377→        self.count += other.count;
   378→    }
   379→}
   380→```
   381→
   382→This enables `SumTree<StatusEntry>` to support:
   383→- O(log N) lookup by `RepoPath` using `tree.get(&path, &())`
   384→- Total count in O(1) from the root summary
   385→- Ordered iteration for building the flat list
   386→
   387→---
   388→
   389→## Part 2 — `crates/project`: GitStore and Repository Entities
   390→
   391→`git_store.rs` is the largest single file in the git stack (~2,400 lines). It
   392→translates raw `GitRepository` operations into a GPUI reactive system:
   393→entities, subscriptions, async jobs, events, and protobuf RPC.
   394→
   395→### 2.1  `GitStore` struct
   396→
   397→```rust
   398→pub struct GitStore {
   399→    // ── Mode ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   400→    mode: GitStoreMode,
   401→
   402→    // ── Repository registry ────────────────────────────────────────
   403→    repositories: HashMap<RepositoryId, Entity<Repository>>,
   404→    active_repository: Option<Entity<Repository>>,
   405→    next_repository_id: RepositoryId,
   406→
   407→    // ── Buffer diff registry ───────────────────────────────────────
   408→    /// For each open buffer in a git repo, we track two diffs:
   409→    /// (unstaged_diff, uncommitted_diff).
   410→    /// unstaged:    working tree vs. index     (base = index text)
   411→    /// uncommitted: working tree vs. HEAD      (base = HEAD text)
   412→    buffer_diffs: HashMap<BufferId,
   413→        (Option<Entity<BufferDiff>>, Option<Entity<BufferDiff>>)>,
   414→
   415→    // ── Shared state ───────────────────────────────────────────────
   416→    worktree_store: Entity<WorktreeStore>,
   417→    buffer_store: Entity<BufferStore>,
   418→    project_path_for_repo_path: HashMap<RepositoryId,
   419→        Box<dyn Fn(&RepoPath) -> Option<ProjectPath>>>,
   420→
   421→    // ── AskPass credential infrastructure ─────────────────────────
   422→    askpass_delegates: HashMap<u64, AskPassDelegate>,
   423→    next_askpass_id: u64,
   424→
   425→    // ── Serialization ──────────────────────────────────────────────
   426→    _subscriptions: Vec<Subscription>,
   427→}
   428→
   429→enum GitStoreMode {
   430→    /// Local project: we own real Repository entities.
   431→    Local,
   432→    /// Remote project: operations forwarded to host via RPC.
   433→    Remote { upstream: AnyProtoClient },
   434→}
   435→```
   436→
   437→#### `RepositoryId`
   438→
   439→```rust
   440→pub struct RepositoryId(u64);
   441→```
   442→
   443→A monotonically incrementing integer assigned when `GitStore` creates a new
   444→`Repository` entity. It is stable for the lifetime of the project session
   445→(but not persisted across restarts).
   446→
   447→#### How `GitStore` is created
   448→
   449→`GitStore` is constructed inside `Project::local()`:
   450→
   451→```rust
   452→let git_store = cx.new(|cx| {
   453→    GitStore::local(worktree_store.clone(), buffer_store.clone(), cx)
   454→});
   455→```
   456→
   457→In `GitStore::local()`, the constructor subscribes to `WorktreeStore`:
   458→
   459→```rust
   460→let worktree_sub = cx.subscribe(&worktree_store, |this, _, event, cx| {
   461→    match event {
   462→        WorktreeStoreEvent::WorktreeUpdatedGitRepositories(worktree_id, repos) => {
   463→            this.update_repositories(worktree_id, repos, cx);
   464→        }
   465→        _ => {}
   466→    }
   467→});
   468→
   469→let buffer_sub = cx.subscribe(&buffer_store, |this, _, event, cx| {
   470→    match event {
   471→        BufferStoreEvent::BufferOpened(buffer) => {
   472→            this.create_buffer_diffs_for(&buffer, cx);
   473→        }
   474→        BufferStoreEvent::BufferClosed(buffer_id) => {
   475→            this.buffer_diffs.remove(&buffer_id);
   476→        }
   477→    }
   478→});
   479→
   480→self._subscriptions = vec![worktree_sub, buffer_sub];
   481→```
   482→
   483→### 2.2  `GitStoreEvent` — the event type
   484→
   485→```rust
   486→pub enum GitStoreEvent {
   487→    /// A Repository entity was added or its active branch changed.
   488→    ActiveRepositoryChanged,
   489→    /// Repository status entries changed (files staged/unstaged/modified).
   490→    StatusesChanged(RepositoryId),
   491→    /// A commit completed.
   492→    CommitCompleted { repository_id: RepositoryId, was_amend: bool },
   493→    /// Repository HEAD moved (branch switch, commit, uncommit).
   494→    BranchChanged,
   495→    /// Buffer diffs were recalculated.
   496→    DiffChanged { buffer_id: BufferId },
   497→}
   498→```
   499→
   500→The `GitPanel` subscribes to these events in its constructor. The pattern is:
   501→
   502→```rust
   503→let _git_sub = cx.subscribe(&git_store, |this, _store, event, cx| {
   504→    match event {
   505→        GitStoreEvent::StatusesChanged(_) => this.rebuild_entries(cx),
   506→        GitStoreEvent::ActiveRepositoryChanged => {
   507→            this.selected_ix = None;
   508→            this.rebuild_entries(cx);
   509→        }
   510→        GitStoreEvent::CommitCompleted { .. } => {
   511→            this.commit_editor.update(cx, |ed, cx| ed.set_text("", cx));
   512→        }
   513→        GitStoreEvent::BranchChanged => cx.notify(),
   514→        _ => {}
   515→    }
   516→});
   517→```
   518→
   519→### 2.3  Repository Discovery
   520→
   521→When the worktree scanner discovers a `.git` directory, it reports it via
   522→`WorktreeStoreEvent::WorktreeUpdatedGitRepositories`. `GitStore` then calls:
   523→
   524→```rust
   525→fn update_repositories(
   526→    &mut self,
   527→    worktree_id: WorktreeId,
   528→    discovered: Vec<RepositoryEntry>,  // from WorktreeSnapshot
   529→    cx: &mut Context<Self>,
   530→) {
   531→    for entry in discovered {
   532→        if self.repositories.values().any(|r| r.read(cx).dot_git_path == entry.work_directory) {
   533→            // Already known; refresh its state.
   534→            continue;
   535→        }
   536→        // Create a new Repository entity.
   537→        let repo_entity = cx.new(|cx| {
   538→            Repository::new(entry, worktree_id, cx)
   539→        });
   540→
   541→        let id = self.next_repository_id.post_increment();
   542→        self.repositories.insert(id, repo_entity.clone());
   543→
   544→        // When the Repository entity changes, re-emit a GitStoreEvent.
   545→        let sub = cx.observe(&repo_entity, |this, _, cx| {
   546→            cx.emit(GitStoreEvent::StatusesChanged(id));
   547→        });
   548→        self._subscriptions.push(sub);
   549→
   550→        // If this is the first repository or matches the active worktree,
   551→        // set it as active.
   552→        if self.active_repository.is_none() {
   553→            self.active_repository = Some(repo_entity);
   554→            cx.emit(GitStoreEvent::ActiveRepositoryChanged);
   555→        }
   556→    }
   557→}
   558→```
   559→
   560→The path-conversion closure `project_path_for_repo_path` is also set here:
   561→
   562→```rust
   563→self.project_path_for_repo_path.insert(id, Box::new(move |repo_path| {
   564→    // Map repo-relative path → (worktree_id, worktree-relative path)
   565→    let worktree_relative = repo_root.join(repo_path).strip_prefix(&worktree_root)?;
   566→    Some(ProjectPath { worktree_id, path: Arc::from(worktree_relative) })
   567→}));
   568→```
   569→
   570→This closure is called by the `GitPanel` when the user opens a file from the
   571→changed-files list.
   572→
   573→### 2.4  `Repository` entity — per-repository state
   574→
   575→```rust
   576→pub struct Repository {
   577→    // ── Identity ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
   578→    pub worktree_id: WorktreeId,
   579→    pub dot_git_path: PathBuf,      // abs path to .git dir
   580→
   581→    // ── Status ground truth ────────────────────────────────────────
   582→    pub statuses: SumTree<StatusEntry>,
   583→    pub snapshot: RepositorySnapshot,
   584→
   585→    // ── Git backend ────────────────────────────────────────────────
   586→    git_repo: Arc<dyn GitRepository>,
   587→
   588→    // ── Async job queue ────────────────────────────────────────────
   589→    job_sender: mpsc::UnboundedSender<GitJob>,
   590→    active_jobs: HashMap<JobId, Task<()>>,  // tasks kept alive
   591→    next_job_id: JobId,
   592→
   593→    // ── Optimistic state for the UI ────────────────────────────────
   594→    /// Paths with pending staging/unstaging operations.
   595→    /// The panel shows these as if already staged/unstaged.
   596→    pending_ops: HashMap<RepoPath, PendingOp>,
   597→
   598→    // ── Commit editor state ────────────────────────────────────────
   599→    commit_message_buffer: Option<Entity<Buffer>>,

input.content:
# Git Abstraction Layer

| Field        | Value       |
| ------------ | ----------- |
| **Type**     | `feature`   |
| **Priority** | `p1`        |
| **Parent**   | —           |
| **Created**  | 2026-03-13  |

## Summary

Build a three-layer git abstraction for cupel modeled after Zed's git crates: a pure git2/CLI wrapper (`src/git/`), GPUI entity wrappers (`GitStore`, `Repository`), and per-buffer diff state (`BufferDiff`). This is the foundational infrastructure that all git UI features depend on.

## Child Issues

### Layer 1 — Pure Git Abstraction

- [ ] [Module scaffolding](feature-git-abstraction-layer--module-scaffolding.md)
- [ ] [Data types](feature-git-abstraction-layer--data-types.md)
- [ ] [Error types](feature-git-abstraction-layer--error-types.md)
- [ ] [Repository trait](feature-git-abstraction-layer--repository-trait.md)
- [ ] [Real repo read-only methods](feature-git-abstraction-layer--real-repo-readonly.md)
- [ ] [Real repo mutating methods](feature-git-abstraction-layer--real-repo-mutating.md)
- [ ] [Fake repository test double](feature-git-abstraction-layer--fake-repository.md)

### Layer 2 — GitStore & Repository Entities

- [ ] [Repository entity](feature-git-abstraction-layer--repository-entity.md)
- [ ] [GitStore entity](feature-git-abstraction-layer--git-store-entity.md)
- [ ] [Events & optimistic UI](feature-git-abstraction-layer--events-optimistic-ui.md)

### Layer 3 — Buffer Diff

- [ ] [BufferDiff entity](feature-git-abstraction-layer--buffer-diff-entity.md)
- [ ] [Secondary hunk status](feature-git-abstraction-layer--secondary-hunk-status.md)

## User Story

As a cupel developer, I want a layered git abstraction so that git operations are testable, async-safe, and decoupled from the UI layer.

## Dependency Graph

```
#1 (scaffolding)
├── #2 (data types)  ─┐
├── #3 (error types) ─┤
│                      ├── #4 (trait) ──┬── #5 (real readonly) ── #6 (real mutating) ──┐
│                      │                └── #7 (fake repo) ────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                                                                ├── #8 (repo entity) ── #9 (git store) ── #10 (events/optimistic)
└── #11 (buffer diff, only needs imara-diff dep) ── #12 (secondary hunk status, also needs #10)
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `src/git/` module compiles with all types, traits, and implementations.
- [ ] `GitRepository` trait has ~40 methods covering status, branch, blame, staging, commit, push/pull, stash, log, and conflict resolution.
- [ ] `RealGitRepository` wraps git2 for local ops and shells out to `git` CLI for network ops.
- [ ] `FakeGitRepository` enables fully in-memory testing without a real repo.
- [ ] `Repository` GPUI entity serializes git ops through an async job queue.
- [ ] `GitStore` GPUI entity acts as a repository registry with event emission.
- [ ] `BufferDiff` entity keeps diff hunks up-to-date as buffers change.
- [ ] `SecondaryHunkStatus` enables staging-aware diff display.
- [ ] All layers compile and pass tests independently.

## Design Considerations

- Follow Zed's layered architecture: `git` (pure) → `project` (entities) → `git_ui` (rendering).
- The pure layer has no GPUI, no async runtime — just synchronous trait methods.
- `git2::Repository` is not `Sync`; wrap in `Mutex` inside `RealGitRepository`.
- Network operations (push/pull/fetch) use `git` CLI subprocess, not git2.
- Use `imara-diff` for the diff algorithm (replaces `similar`).
- The `env: &HashMap<String, String>` parameter pattern on mutating methods supports future credential helper injection.

## Out of Scope

- Git UI panel (GitPanel, ProjectDiff, ConflictView) — that's a separate epic.
- AskPass credential helper binary — can use empty env for local-only operations initially.
- Remote mode (`GitStoreMode::Remote`) and protobuf RPC — local-only for Phase 0.
- Worktree scanner integration — repositories will be added manually for now.

## Reference

- Architecture reference: [.epic/git.md](../.epic/git.md)

# Module Scaffolding

| Field        | Value                                                              |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Type**     | `feature`                                                          |
| **Priority** | `p1`                                                               |
| **Parent**   | [Git Abstraction Layer](feature-git-abstraction-layer.md)          |
| **Created**  | 2026-03-13                                                         |

## Summary

Create the `src/git/` module layout with `mod.rs` and empty submodule files, and add the `git2` and `imara-diff` dependencies to `Cargo.toml`. This is the foundation that all other git issues build on.

## User Story

As a developer, I want the module structure and dependencies in place so that subsequent issues can implement types and traits without setup overhead.

## Detailed Description

Replace the current `src/git.rs` stub with a `src/git/` directory containing:

```
src/git/
├── mod.rs           # re-exports
├── repository.rs    # GitRepository trait (issue #4)
├── real_repo.rs     # RealGitRepository (issues #5, #6)
├── fake_repo.rs     # FakeGitRepository (issue #7)
├── types.rs         # data types (issue #2)
├── status.rs        # StatusEntry + related types
├── error.rs         # GitError (issue #3)
└── diff.rs          # BufferDiff types (issue #11)
```

Add to `Cargo.toml` dependencies:
- `git2` — libgit2 bindings
- `imara-diff` — diff algorithm

Wire `mod git` into `src/lib.rs`.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `src/git.rs` is replaced by `src/git/mod.rs` with submodule declarations.
- [ ] All submodule files exist (can be empty or contain minimal placeholder structs).
- [ ] `git2` and `imara-diff` are added to `[dependencies]` in `Cargo.toml`.
- [ ] `cargo check` passes.
- [ ] `src/lib.rs` declares `pub mod git`.

## Design Considerations

- Keep `mod.rs` re-exports minimal for now; each issue will add its own public items.
- Use the same module naming as Zed's `crates/git/src/` for familiarity.
- The `diff.rs` module is for buffer-diff types used in Layer 3; it only needs the `imara-diff` dep from this issue.

## Out of Scope

- Implementing any types or traits — that's issues #2–#7 and #11–#12.
- Adding GPUI-related dependencies — those belong in Layer 2 issues.

## Test Guidance

**Primary category:** Integration tests

**What to test:**

- `cargo check` passes with the new module structure.
- All modules are importable from `cupel::git::*`.

**Boundary conditions:**

- Ensure no circular dependencies between submodules.

**Infrastructure needs:**

- None beyond standard `cargo`.

## Definition of Done

- [ ] Implementation is complete and builds without errors.
- [ ] All new and existing tests pass in CI.
- [ ] Code has been reviewed and approved by at least one other engineer.
- [ ] Changes are documented — at minimum, meaningful commit messages and inline comments where intent is non-obvious.
- [ ] All success criteria listed in the issue are met.
